name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization

governance.decision_making: Consensus required for all major decisions

strategic_priorities: Sustained military support for Ukraine and its eventual NATO integration path
Achieving 2% GDP defense spending — now 2.5% target under discussion for 2030
Eastern flank reinforcement: battalion battle groups in Baltic states and Poland
Indo-Pacific partnerships with Australia, Japan, South Korea, and New Zealand
Counter-hybrid warfare and cyber defense capacity building
Reducing dependence on US for critical munitions, air defense, and logistics
summary: NATO remains the world's most powerful military alliance, but faces structural stress from divergent threat perceptions, the long-term burden of Ukraine support, and US pressure for European allies to sharply increase defense spending. Finland (2023) and Sweden (2024) completed the alliance's most significant recent enlargement, closing the Nordic gap. The alliance's center of gravity is shifting eastward as Poland and Baltic states drive the deterrence agenda.
recent_developments: 23 of 32 allies met the 2% GDP defense spending target in 2025, up from 10 in 2021; Poland leads at 4.0% of GDP
NATO established a new High Visibility Presence in Ukraine short of full membership, providing interoperability and training missions
The Hague Summit (June 2025) endorsed a new 'NATO 2030+' Strategic Concept updating the 2022 Madrid document to reflect expanded membership
US-Europe tensions over burdensharing intensified in early 2026; European allies accelerated domestic defense procurement
NATO Integrated Air and Missile Defense network upgraded with additional Patriot and SAMP/T batteries on the eastern flank
risks: US political shifts creating uncertainty about Article 5 commitment credibility
Turkey-Greece bilateral tensions periodically disrupting alliance cohesion
Hungary blocking or delaying alliance decisions on Ukraine
Defense industrial base insufficient to sustain high-tempo munitions consumption
